summary: I am Quinton E. Baker, born in Greenville, North Carolina, and I grew up as the youngest of four children in a working-class family. Throughout my youth, I was aware that I was different, especially because of my interests and later my identity as a gay Black man. I was active in the Chapel Hill civil rights movement and have shared my experiences in an interview recorded in 2002 for the Southern Oral History Program. My relationship with my father was complicated because he struggled to accept my differences, but I maintained my independence and identity despite challenges.

Chapel Hill, North Carolina, is where I became active in the civil rights movement as a gay Black man. The area, including the Triangle region, has a significant history of gay, lesbian, bisexual, and transgender communities throughout the twentieth century, which was the focus of the Southern Oral History Program's project I contributed to. My involvement in Chapel Hill's civil rights activities was part of a broader struggle for equality and recognition in this progressive university town. The interviews and documentation of this history are preserved at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.
cues: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

summary: The Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill conducted an interview with me on February 23, 2002, as part of their Gay and Lesbian Southern History Project. This program focuses on documenting the experiences of gay, lesbian, bisexual, and transgender individuals in Chapel Hill and the surrounding region over the twentieth century. My interview was recorded to contribute to this important historical archive, which is stored in the Southern Historical Collection at Wilson Library. The program plays a vital role in preserving the voices and stories of marginalized communities in the South.
cues: Gay and Lesbian Southern History Project
University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
summary: The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill is the institution that houses the Southern Oral History Program, which recorded my interview in 2002. The university's library, specifically Wilson Library, holds the Southern Historical Collection where my oral history is archived. UNC Chapel Hill has been central to documenting the civil rights and LGBTQ history of the region, including the experiences of people like me who were active in the Chapel Hill community. This university has played a key role in preserving and sharing the stories of marginalized groups in North Carolina.

I was born and raised in Greenville, North Carolina, where I spent the first eighteen years of my life. Greenville was a small town of about 21,000 people when I was growing up, and I attended the segregated C.M. Eppes High School there. My parents were laborers, and I worked various jobs including shining shoes downtown to avoid working in the tobacco fields. Growing up in Greenville shaped much of my early experiences with race, segregation, and identity before I became involved in civil rights activities in Chapel Hill.

I attended C.M. Eppes High School in Greenville, North Carolina, which was the all-black high school during segregation. This was where I began to understand more about my identity as a gay man, especially through my friendships with openly gay students like Lester. The school environment reflected the segregated society of the time, but it was also a place where I developed my sense of self despite the challenges of being different. My experiences there were part of growing up in the segregated South before desegregation changed the community dynamics.
cues: friendship with openly gay student Lester
